title: Quiz: The NISQ Era Overview
description: Test your understanding of NISQ devices and their characteristics

questions.question: What does NISQ stand for?
questions.explanation: NISQ stands for Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum, coined by John Preskill to describe current quantum devices.

questions.question: What is the primary constraint in NISQ computing?

options.text: Circuit depth due to noise and decoherence

questions.explanation: Circuit depth is limited by gate errors and decoherence time, making this the primary constraint for NISQ algorithms.

questions.question: Why are variational algorithms preferred in the NISQ era?

options.text: They use shallow circuits and hybrid classical-quantum optimization

questions.explanation: Variational algorithms use shallow, parameterized circuits optimized by classical computers, making them well-suited for noisy devices.

questions.question: Full fault-tolerant error correction is currently available on NISQ devices.
questions.explanation: False. NISQ devices do not have full error correction. Instead, error mitigation techniques are used to reduce noise impact.

questions.question: What is a key advantage of trapped ion quantum computers?

options.text: All-to-all qubit connectivity and high fidelity

questions.explanation: Trapped ion systems offer all-to-all connectivity (no SWAP gates needed) and very high gate fidelities.

questions.question: What type of quantum advantage has been demonstrated?

options.text: Computational supremacy for specific sampling problems

questions.explanation: Computational supremacy has been demonstrated for specific sampling tasks (like random circuit sampling), but practical advantage for useful problems is not yet proven.

questions.question: What happens if circuit depth exceeds NISQ limits?
questions.explanation: Exceeding depth limits causes accumulated errors and decoherence to dominate, making outputs essentially random noise.

questions.question: Which are recommended practices for NISQ algorithm design? (Select all)
questions.explanation: NISQ algorithms should use shallow circuits, apply error mitigation, and compile to native gates. Never ignore noise—it's the defining characteristic of NISQ.
